title: Business Support Organizations Available to Help You
objectives: Identify SBDC, SBA, chambers of commerce, and economic development agencies relevant to their business
Describe what each organization offers (counseling, funding access, networking, training)
Explain how to access these resources and what to expect from the engagement

contentBlocks.title: You Don't Have to Figure This Out Alone
contentBlocks.content:
Having established the legal, financial, and cultural foundations in earlier modules, this final module addresses the support ecosystem most founders never hear about. A Colombian founder spent $4,500 on a business consultant to develop a market entry plan for Central Florida. Three months later, she discovered the SBDC at UCF offers the same service — for free.

What does that mean for you? The U.S. has an entire ecosystem of business support organizations funded by federal, state, and local government. Most welcome international entrepreneurs, many have bilingual staff, and almost all are free or low-cost. You're not asking for charity — these resources were created specifically for founders like you.

contentBlocks.title: The Key Organizations — What Each One Does
contentBlocks.content:
SBDC (Small Business Development Center) at UCF

One of your most valuable free resources. SBDC advisors provide one-on-one business counseling at no cost : ongoing help with business planning, market research, financial analysis, and operational strategy. Your first appointment is a 60-90 minute intake, followed by a tailored action plan and unlimited follow-up sessions. They also run workshops (free to $50) on business plans, financial management, and government contracting. Find them at floridasbdc.org.

SBA (Small Business Administration)

The federal agency supporting small businesses. Key resources:

  • SBA.gov . Free guides on formation, licensing, taxes, and capital access
  • SBA lending programs . Favorable terms, though eligibility for non-citizens is limited (see Module 2)
  • Government contracting assistance . Programs helping small businesses compete for federal contracts

Important: SBA counseling and educational resources are available regardless of citizenship status. Lending programs typically require permanent residency. Don't let lending eligibility stop you from using their free resources.

Orlando Economic Partnership (OEP)

The regional economic development organization : offers site selection assistance, market intelligence, introductions to local business leaders, and tax incentive information. Free.

Chambers of Commerce

Membership-based organizations ($200-$500/year) providing networking, advocacy, and credibility:

  • Orlando Regional Chamber . Largest local chamber with broad networking
  • Hispanic Chamber of Metro Orlando . Bilingual resources and mentorship for Hispanic/Latino founders
  • Local chambers (Kissimmee, Winter Park, Sanford) . Community networking with lower fees

contentBlocks.title: Watch Your Money: What This Actually Costs
contentBlocks.content:
Support organization costs as of early 2026:

  • SBDC business counseling: Free (unlimited sessions)
  • SBDC workshops: Free to $50 per session
  • SBA.gov resources and guides: Free
  • Orlando Economic Partnership consultation: Free
  • Chamber of Commerce membership: $200–$500/year
  • SCORE mentoring: Free (volunteer business mentors)
  • Prospera programs: Free to low-cost

Compare that to private consulting: $150–$350/hour for a business consultant, or $3,000–$10,000 for a market entry strategy that the SBDC develops with you at no cost. Always check the free options first.

contentBlocks.title: Common Mistakes International Founders Make — Resources
contentBlocks.content:
• Not knowing about free resources (SBDC, SBA counseling). Most international founders assume quality business advice costs money. The U.S. has an entire taxpayer-funded system designed to help you for free.
  • Paying consultants for advice available through government programs. Before you hire any consultant, check if the SBDC or SBA covers that topic first. You might save $5,000 or more.
  • Not leveraging UCF BIP and chamber connections. These organizations exist to connect you with the right people. A warm introduction from UCF BIP or a chamber contact carries more weight than a cold email.

contentBlocks.title: Before You Hire a Business Consultant
contentBlocks.content:
Before spending money on private consulting, complete this checklist:

  1. Have you scheduled a free SBDC counseling session? (floridasbdc.org)
  2. Have you reviewed the SBA.gov resources for your specific question?
  3. Have you attended at least one free SBDC or chamber workshop?
  4. Have you asked UCF BIP if they can connect you with the resource you need?

If the answer to all four is yes and you still need specialized expertise, then a consultant may be warranted. But start with the free options. Many founders discover they don't need the consultant at all.

questions.stem: Which organization provides free, ongoing one-on-one business counseling to international founders in Central Florida?
questions.explanation: The Florida SBDC (Small Business Development Center) at UCF provides free one-on-one business counseling. This is an ongoing relationship, not a one-time meeting. Advisors help with business planning, market research, financial analysis, and strategy at no cost.

questions.stem: An international founder wants help with market research and a business plan. What should they do FIRST?
options: Hire a business consultant specializing in market entry
Schedule a free appointment with the Florida SBDC at UCF
Join the Orlando Regional Chamber of Commerce
Apply for an SBA loan to fund the research
questions.explanation: The SBDC should be your first stop for market research and business planning support. Their advisors provide exactly this service at no cost. Hiring a consultant for the same work could cost $3,000–$10,000. Always check free resources before paying for private help.

contentBlocks.title: Your Resource Action Plan
contentBlocks.prompt: Look at the organizations described in this lesson. Which two are most relevant to where your business is right now? For each one, write down: (1) what specific help you need from them, (2) when you'll contact them, and (3) what you'll ask in your first meeting. If you've already been paying a consultant for services that might be available free, note that too.

contentBlocks.title: Your Support Network Map
contentBlocks.instructions:
You just learned about several free and low-cost support organizations. Now pick the three that matter most for where your business is right now — not where you hope to be in two years.

  • Organization 1: ___
    Why it fits your current stage: ___
    Your first action (this week): ___
  • Organization 2: ___
    Why it fits your current stage: ___
    Your first action (this week): ___
  • Organization 3: ___
    Why it fits your current stage: ___
    Your first action (this week): ___

Be specific. "Contact the SBDC" is not an action. "Email floridasbdc.org to schedule an intake meeting about my market research gaps" is.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.content: Central Florida has an entire ecosystem built to help businesses succeed — and most of it is free. The SBDC at UCF should be your first call for business planning and market research. The SBA offers federal resources and guides. Chambers of commerce provide networking and credibility. Use these resources before you spend money on private consultants. The founders who succeed fastest are the ones who use every available advantage.
